Computed tomography and magnetic resonance 
imaging
Computed tomography (CT) is the diagnostic imaging 
technique of choice for evaluation of the ear, skull and 
spine. It is an essential tool that is increasingly available 
in veterinary practice (see Chapter 9). Prognosis and treat- (c)
ment decisions can be better determined with the use of 
this imaging technique as it allows a full assessment of 
the extent of the disease process (Figure 20.3).
Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is more useful in 
cases with CNS disease. Soft tissue abnormalities can be 
more clearly visualized with this modality. MRI shows 
abnormalities of the tympanic bullae, intracranial soft tis-
sue lesions and anatomical/congenital defects.
Microbiology
In suspected cases of bacterial or fungal infection, culture 
is indicated if possible. Culture can be performed on 
myringotomy samples in cases of otitis media/interna. It 
is also useful when a link with a respiratory infection is 
suspected. (d)

Entropion: In entropion, the hairless margin of the eyelid 
is not visible because the eyelid is turned in, which leads 
haired skin to be apposed against the corneal surface. 
Blepharospasm and excess tearing are seen, and ocular 
pain may occasionally be sufficient to cause inappetence 
and lethargy.
Primary entropion has been reported in guinea pigs. It 
may also be associated with an abnormally small globe. 
Entropion may also be secondary and cicatricial after 
eyelid trauma or chronic inflammation (e.g. conjunctivitis, 
keratoconjunctivitis sicca or trichiasis). The condition 
becomes a problem when the hairs on the eyelid make 
contact with the corneal surface and lead to corneal 
ulceration (trichiasis). Surgical correction by the Hotz–
Celsus procedure is effective. The guinea pig has a thin 
and delicate eyelid, and only approximately 1 mm needs 
to be removed; thus, the surgery requires magnification 
and fine ophthalmic surgical equipment. Hyaluronate injec-
tions to evert the eyelids semi-permanently may also be 
used. If neither treatment option is possible, frequent 
20.14 A guinea pig with ‘fatty eye’. lubrication of the cornea helps to reduce the associated 
clinical signs.
Trichiasis: Trichiasis can occur in guinea pigs, especially 
in the Skinny, Texel and Rex breeds, and can cause con-
junctivitis or corneal ulcers. In neonatal animals of wire-
haired breeds, the wiry hair can curl inward into the eye. 
Lubricating the eyes and surrounding hair frequently after 
birth until the hair grows in a more controlled manner can 
help.
Diseases of the ocular surfaces and conjunctivitis
Corneal ulcers: Corneal ulcers are most commonly trau-
matic in guinea pigs (Figure 20.17). They can occur as a 
result of distichiasis and entropion in Texel guinea pigs. 
Guinea pigs have limited corneal sensitivity and ability to 
produce reflex tears, which gives them a high risk of injury 
from foreign bodies. Straw, hay and plant material can 
traumatize the eye. In some cases, corneal ulcers become 
infected by (most commonly) bacteria and progress to 
ulcerative keratitis with stromal invasion (Figure 20.18).
Corneal ulcers and ulcerative keratitis in rodents may 
be separated into three groups of lesions: epithelial defects 
that are not severely infected; stromal ulcers; and stromal 
20.15 A guinea pig with ‘flesh eye’.
keratitis, which closely resembles stromal abscess. The 
approach to corneal ulcers in guinea pigs is the same as 
that in domestic carnivores. Treatment is initially based on 
tear replacement therapy and antibiotics. In cases of a 
foreign body, the eye should be carefully examined and 
the foreign body removed (under local or general anaes-
thesia) before the resulting ulcer is treated. Hay poke can 
result in severe inflammation and require the use of atro-
pine locally and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs 
(e.g. meloxicam) orally. If healing is delayed, an Alger brush 
may be used as in other species. Autologous or heterolo-
gous serum can also be used as a treatment for corneal 
ulcers in guinea pigs.
Stromal abscesses (Figure 20.19) are much less com-
mon in guinea pigs than in rabbits. In cases of severe 
infection (Figure 20.20), aggressive treatment is required.
Vitamin C has been described as having anti-colla-
genase activities. Supplementation during times of ulcera-
tion may be beneficial, especially in the case of melting 
ulcers, which are rare in guinea pigs.

Signs of ocular irritation, blepharospasm and a lack of 
clear sharp reflection of light from the ocular surface are 
highly suggestive of a dry eye. A Schirmer tear test is the 
standard method for evaluating tear production. Many 
studies of this test have been conducted and values are 
very variable in guinea pigs; for example, they could 
depend on breed (see Figure 20.8).
It is unclear whether the immune-mediated lacrimal 
destruction seen in dogs also occurs in guinea pigs. Topical 
ciclosporin has a beneficial effect, so it may be considered 
that the pathological process that reduces tear production

Keratitis: Ocular surface trauma is common in guinea pigs. 
In the acute phase, a corneal epithelial erosion and some-
times stromal damage occurs, but with very little blepha-
rospasm in most cases. Later changes yield a scar with an 
inflammatory cell infiltrate and fibrosis, which often impairs 
vision but rarely leads to obvious signs of ocular surface 
discomfort (Figures 20.24 and 20.25). In the acute phase, 
removal of the inciting foreign body (if present) and topical 
treatment with a broad-spectrum antibiotic such as chlo-
ramphenicol or gentamicin is appropriate. In the chronic 
phase, a superficial keratectomy can be performed. The 
thin cornea makes this procedure delicate, and appropriate 
magnification and instruments are required.
Exposure keratitis is common in guinea pigs (Figure 
20.26). It is most often secondary to facial nerve paralysis 
due to otitis media. It is very often the only sign of otitis 
media, which is likely underdiagnosed. Keratitis can be 
very severe and can be accompanied by severe central 
linear ulceration. Aggressive tear replacement therapy is 
usually effective. Otitis media should be investigated in 
these cases. Listeria spp. keratitis has been reported in  A guinea pig with exposure keratitis due to facial

Dermoid: Dermoids are congenital lesions where skin-like 
tissue is present in an abnormal location. Ocular dermoids 
have been reported in guinea pigs. They are most com-
monly located in the cornea but a corneoscleral dermoid, 
two conjunctival dermoids and a scleral dermoid have 
been reported (Otto et al., 1991; Wappler et al., 2002). The 
scleral dermoid seemed to originate from the sclera and 
the palpebral conjunctiva and consisted mostly of stratified 
squamous keratinized and variably pigmented epithelium 
overlying an irregular dermis containing hair, sweat glands 
and sebaceous glands. Rarely, cartilage or bone is seen 
within the congenitally malpositioned tissue. Irritation from 
abnormal hair may cause conjunctivitis and corneal ulcera-
tion. Surgical resection is curative but leaves a corneal 
epithelial defect. Topical antibiotics should be prescribed 
for 10 days after surgery.

can be encountered in guinea pigs (Figure 20.27). Corneal

Another type of corneal dystrophy or degeneration 
usually appears as small dense white punctuate opacities. 
It has been proposed that bacteria in faecal material con-
vert urinary urea to ammonia, which then comes into 
contact with the cornea and causes necrosis of the corneal 
epithelium. This eventually results in scarring and miner-
alization of the stroma, with dysplasia of the overlying 
epithelium. When the animal’s environment is improved, 
the lesions may also improve. The prognosis for retaining 
vision is good but leucomas (whitish corneal scars) are a 
common consequence.
